message.content: Initialize a hash table of size $O(n)$. When an element $a_i$ arrives, check whether $\frac{T^{1/3}-3a_i}{2}$ is an integer; if so, look up this value in the hash table and answer yes if it is found, otherwise no. Afterwards, always insert $a_i$ into the hash table. Alternatively, upon the arrival of the element, check whether it is already in the table and, if so, answer yes, otherwise no. Then insert into the hash the value $\frac{T^{1/3}-2a_i}{3}$ if it is an integer. Another option is, upon the arrival of an element, to look up $T^{1/3}-3a_i$ and answer yes if it is found, and then insert $2a_i$. All operations are performed in expected constant time per key.

We use perfect hashing of size $O(n)$ that contains all the keys, built in expected time $O(n)$. Given a query $x$, we check whether $(T^{\frac{1}{3}} - 3x)/2$ is an integer; if so, we search for it in the hash table and answer yes if it is found, and otherwise no. The check is done in $O(1)$ time in the worst case. Alternatively, one can insert only the (up to $n$) integer keys among $(T^{\frac{1}{3}} - 2a_i)/2$ in expected time $O(n)$. Given a query $x$, we check whether its value is in the table and answer yes if it is.

In this part we treat the elements and their weights as ordered pairs $(x_i, w_i)$ so that at every step we can maintain their association. We find the median of the array by the value $(x_i)$ and denote its index by $m$. We perform a partition of the array around $m$ so that the elements to its left are smaller/equal, and those to its right are larger/equal. We sum the weights $(w_i)$ of the elements to the left of the median and denote this sum by $T \equiv \sum_{i<m} w_i$. If $T+S \ge \frac{1}{2}$, we copy the elements to the left of the median into a new array and make a recursive call on it with the accumulated weight $S$. Otherwise (i.e., if $T+S < \frac{1}{2}$), if $T+S+w_m \ge \frac{1}{2}$, we return $x_m$ as the desired value. Otherwise (i.e., if $T+S+w_m < \frac{1}{2}$), we copy the elements to the right of the median into a new array and make a recursive call on it with the accumulated weight $S+T+w_m$. The running time is $\Theta(n)$.

Solution to the previous part: In this section we operate on the elements and their weights as ordered pairs $(x_i,w_i)$ so that at every step we can keep them linked. Find the median of the array by the value $x_i$, and denote its index by $m$. Perform a partition of the array around $m$ so that the elements to its left are smaller/equal and those to its right are larger/equal. Sum the weights $(w_i)$ of the elements to the left of the median; denote this sum $T \equiv \sum_{i<m} w_i$. If $T+S \ge \tfrac{1}{2}$: copy the elements to the left of the median into a new array, and make the recursive call on it with the accumulated weight $S$. Otherwise ($T+S < \tfrac{1}{2}$), if $T+S+w_m \ge \tfrac{1}{2}$: return $x_m$ as the desired value. Otherwise ($T+S+w_m < \tfrac{1}{2}$): copy the elements to the right of the median into a new array, and make the recursive call on it with the accumulated weight $S+T+w_m$. The running time is $\Theta(n)$. In this part there are no weights, only values. The solution template is identical to part (a), except for the required adjustments to the checks: $0$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ and the equality in the conditions switches sides. Sum the values of the elements $(a_i)$ to the left of the median; denote this sum $T \equiv \sum_{i<m} a_i$. If $T+S > 0$: copy the elements to the left of the median into a new array, and make the recursive call on it with the accumulated sum $S$. Otherwise ($T+S \le 0$), if $T+S+a_m > 0$: return $a_m$ as the desired value. Otherwise ($T+S+a_m \le 0$): copy the elements to the right of the median into a new array, and make the recursive call on it with the accumulated sum $S+T+a_m$.

Initialize an output array of size $n$. Note that the minimal element can be found among the first $k+1$ elements. Build a min-heap from the first $k+1$ elements, and then, for $n$ iterations, extract the minimum from the heap into the next position of the output array and insert the next element of the input array into the heap (unless we have already reached the end of the input array, in which case we do not insert). The heap size is always $O(k)$, so each operation takes $O(\log k)$, and the total time complexity is $O(n \log k)$.

We prove a lower bound via a comparison tree. We bound how many leaves can be in any comparison tree. Take a sorted array and partition it into blocks of size $\sqrt{n}$. Note that if we arrange each block in any permutation we choose, the resulting array satisfies the problem’s condition. Each such array must lead to a different leaf, and therefore there are $(\sqrt{n}!)^{\sqrt{n}}$ leaves in any comparison tree. Hence at least $\log\big((\sqrt{n}!)^{\sqrt{n}}\big)=\Omega(n \log n)$ comparisons are required.

We use an AVL tree ordered by the angle magnitude. Insertion, deletion, and search take $O(\log n)$. To implement deletion of the elements on an arc, perform a split at the point $\theta_{1}$ to obtain $T_{1}, T_{2}$. Then split $T_{2}$ at the point $\theta_{2}$ to obtain $T_{1}^{\prime}$, which contains the points between $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$. Finally, perform a join between $T_{2}^{\prime}$ and $T_{1}$. If $\theta_{1} > \theta_{2}$, return the complementary tree $T_{1}^{\prime}$. To support the Rotate operation, maintain an auxiliary variable that accumulates the angles modulo $2\pi$ and correct all angles by subtracting this variable. The operation runs in $O(1)$.

We prove this for the implementation \texttt{build\_heap}. Observe that we perform \texttt{heapify\_down} on a node if and only if we have visited its left child. We prove that we visit each node exactly once by induction on the levels. For the bottom level (leaves) this is immediate due to the loop structure. The induction step follows from the fact that we visit a node only after visiting its left child, and by the induction hypothesis this happens once. Complexity analysis: the running time sums to $O(n)$.

Counterexample. For example, the binary heap $[7,6,5,4,3,2,1]$. The idea behind the counterexample is that we perform \texttt{heapify\_down} on the root at a fairly early stage of the algorithm (and we do so only once), and therefore, if the minimum has not been visited up to that point, it will never reach the root.

Proof. We perform \texttt{heapify\_down} on a node (immediately after we have performed \texttt{heapify\_down} on its right child) only after we have performed \texttt{heapify\_down} on its children. As we saw in class, this property guarantees that after \texttt{heapify\_down} the subtree rooted at that node necessarily satisfies the heap property.

We build an AVL tree from the sorted array of the numbers $1$ to $n$. If $i$ is in the tree, then return $i$; otherwise, insert $i$ into the tree, find its successor $x$, and if $x>j$ return that there are no free rooms (remember to remove $i$ if we inserted it). To compute the number of free rooms, insert $i$ and $j$ into the tree (if they are not already there), compute $\operatorname{Rank}(j)-\operatorname{Rank}(i)$, and return this difference after offsetting for the elements we inserted; at the end, delete them. To perform $\texttt{set}(i,b)$: if $b=1$ insert $i$ into the tree (if it does not exist), and if $b=0$ delete $i$ from the tree.

In the worst case, when comparing all the elements to the pivot element, at least $n/6$ elements are discarded and we are left with at most $5n/6$ elements. Therefore, the recurrence is $T(n) = T(n/6) + T(5n/6) + O(n)$, whose solution is $T(n) = O(n \log n)$.

In the worst case, at least $2 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{n}{6} + 3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3}\right) \cdot \frac{n}{6} = \frac{7n}{36}$ elements are eliminated, and we are left with at most $\frac{29n}{36}$ elements. Therefore, the recurrence is $T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{6}\right) + T\!\left(\frac{29n}{36}\right) + O(n)$, whose solution is $T(n) = O(n)$.
In Example 1, from $0$ to $\infty$, one can see that it is a ``nice'' tree but not a search tree.

We prove a lower bound by a reduction from sorting: given input $a_0, \dots, a_{n-1}$, we construct a ``nice'' path of size $n$ such that the node at depth $i$ contains the element $a_i$. For every $1 \le i \le n-1$, if the value at the node at depth $i$ is smaller than its parent, we place it as the left child; otherwise, as the right child. The reduction takes $O(n)$, and therefore by the sorting lower bound,
$T(n) = \Omega(n \log n) - O(n) = \Omega(n \log n)$.

We build a perfect binary tree of depth $\lceil \log n \rceil + 1$ containing the value $-\infty$ at all nodes. Place the input elements on any $n$ distinct right leaves; the tree remains ``nice'' because all elements are greater than or equal to minus infinity. Sorting this ``nice'' perfect tree yields a sorting of the original input in time $T(n) = \Omega(n \log n)$.

Maintain a doubly linked list $L$ of the snake’s segments and a hash table $H$. At each step:
\begin{enumerate}
\item Compute $q = L.\mathrm{getLast()}$ (the old tail), perform $H.\mathrm{delete}(q)$ and $L.\mathrm{deleteLast()}$.
\item Check whether the new head $p$ collides with the walls or whether $H.\mathrm{find}(p)$ succeeds (self-collision); if so, terminate.
\item Otherwise, perform $L.\mathrm{insertFirst}(p)$ and $H.\mathrm{insert}(p)$.
\end{enumerate}
Complexity: $O(1)$ expected time.

Solution to the previous part: Maintain a doubly linked list $L$ of the snake’s segments and a hash table $H$. At each step:
1. Compute $q = L.getLast()$ (the old tail), perform $H.delete(q)$ and $L.deleteLast()$.
2. Check whether the new head $p$ collides with the walls or whether $H.find(p)$ returns true (self-collision); if so—we terminate.
3. Otherwise, perform $L.insertFirst(p)$ and $H.insert(p)$.
Complexity: $O(1)$ expected time.

Current part: The solution is identical to part (a), except that $H$ will be a balanced binary search tree (e.g., an AVL tree) with a lexicographic order on points. Complexity: $O(\log n)$ worst-case time.

Lemma: Let $x$ be a node of degree $k$ and $y_1,\dots,y_k$ its children in the order of linking. Then the degree of $y_i$ is at least $i-3$.

Proof: Let $x$ be a node of degree $k$ and let $y_1,\dots,y_k$ be its children in the order they were linked. When we linked $y_i$ to $x$, the degree of $x$ was at least $i-1$, and therefore the degree of $y_i$ was at least $i-1$ at the time of linking. The node $y_i$ could have lost up to two children, and hence its current degree is at least $i-3$.

Let \(S_k\) denote the minimal number of nodes in a tree of degree \(k\). From part (a) it follows that \(S_k \ge S_{k-1} + S_{k-3}\). From the hint we get \(S_k \ge c^k\) for some \(c > 1\). For a tree with \(n\) nodes and degree \(k\), we thus have \(n \ge S_k \ge c^k\), hence \(k \le \log_c n\), i.e., \(k = O(\log n)\).

True (circle). The idea is similar to what we saw with the finger search tree in class. The minimum is the node reached by going as far left as possible from the root. Now go upward from the minimum and stop at the first node $x$ whose subtree contains at least $k$ elements. Note that the $k$-th smallest element is in this subtree, and likewise the minimum is in this subtree. Observe that the left child of the root of this subtree contains fewer than $k$ elements, and therefore, by AVL properties, its height is $O(\log k)$. It follows that the height of $x$ is also $O(\log k)$ (the height difference between a child and its parent is $\le 2$). Since both the minimum and the $k$-th smallest element lie in a subtree of height $O(\log k)$, the claim follows.

True (circle). It seems one can place the median at any depth other than zero—say at depth $k$. Place the median on the rightmost path from the root at depth $k$. Ensure that the ancestors of the median are the $k$ smallest elements (in increasing order, of course, to maintain the heap property). In the subtree of the median, place the largest elements while maintaining the heap property (according to the number of elements that need to be placed there)—one can see there are enough elements larger than the median to fill this subtree. Place the remaining elements in the rest of the heap tree (again while maintaining the heap property).

False (circle). We present a counterexample: take the $k$ smallest elements and place them along the leftmost root-to-leaf path in increasing order. Note that we can fill the heap with the remaining elements in a way that preserves the heap property. The $k$-th smallest element sits at depth $k$.

True (circle). In a standard binomial heap there is at most one binomial tree of each degree. The types of binomial trees in the heap are uniquely determined by the number of elements in the heap according to the binary representation. In a heap containing 99 elements, the trees will be \(B_0, B_1, B_5, B_6\).

At each step of the recursion we remove at least $\frac{9}{50}n = 0.3 \cdot \frac{3}{5} n$ elements. Therefore the recurrence is $T(n) = T(n/5) + T(41n/50) + O(n)$. Since $51/50 = 1/5 + 41/50 > 1$, the solution is not linear.

Now, at each step of the recursion we remove at least $\frac{12}{70} n = 0.3 \cdot \frac{4}{7} \cdot n$ elements. Therefore the recurrence is $T(n) = T(n/7) + T\!\big((58/70)n\big) + O(n)$. Since $\frac{68}{70} = \frac{1}{7} + \frac{58}{70} < 1$, the solution is linear.

There are many variations for solving this exercise. One possible solution is to maintain an array of size $n$ (known in advance), indexed by remainders modulo $n$, where each cell holds a linked list with a size field. Initialization: allocate the array and the empty lists — $O(n)$. (This can also be done in constant time as we learned.) Insertion: compute the index via $x \pmod{n}$ (in constant time) and insert into the corresponding linked list while updating the size field. Deletion: given a pointer, deletion from a list is done in constant time; likewise, there is no problem updating the size field because we can compute, given $x$, which list it belongs to in constant time, or alternatively store in each node a pointer to the head of the list, exploiting the fact that there are no unions. Remainder: given the element, access the corresponding array cell and return the list’s size field — $O(1)$.

Initialize two hash tables. Because the range of elements is known in advance and bounded, we can sample a function from a universal family that satisfies the desired properties. One table stores the elements themselves (key: element), while the second counts how many times we have seen a given residue (key: residue; value: counter).

Initialization: Create two hash tables of size $O(n)$ implemented with chaining (with $n$ known in advance).

Insertion: First check the element table; if the element is found, stop. If the element is not present, insert it into the first table and also increment the counter in the second table under the key $x \pmod{2^{n/2}}$. If the key does not exist in the second table, initialize it to $1$.

Deletion: Again check if the element exists. If it does not, stop; otherwise remove it from the first table and decrement the counter in the second table by $1$.

Residue: Query the second table with the key $x \pmod{2^{n/2}}$ and return the value stored there (zero if the key does not exist).

All operations except initialization run in expected $O(1)$ time (and the initialization can be as well).

We can solve this as in Question 3 of Homework 3, using an AVL tree augmented with \texttt{size} fields that stores the occupied seats. In the operation \texttt{Init} we initialize the tree in constant time. In the operation \texttt{Book} we call \( \operatorname{NextMissingAfter}(i) \), obtain \( j \), and insert it into the tree---logarithmic running time. In the operation \texttt{Cancel} we simply delete from the tree---again logarithmic time. Another solution: a simpler one (though with linear initialization time, which is allowed in the question) is to maintain an AVL tree of the free seats instead of the occupied ones.

Initialize an empty array of length $n$ in time $O(1)$, as learned in class. Use a UF structure where the elements are seats and the sets are contiguous blocks of occupied seats, so that each representative stores where the block ends. In the array, store for each seat whether it is free and the UF pointer if it exists. In the Book operation, if seat $i$ is free, mark it as occupied, create for it an element in the UF, and union it with the adjacent occupied seats—the value stored at the new representative will be the maximum of the previous ones and $i$. If seat $i$ is occupied, access its representative in the UF to find where the block ends and occupy the empty seat immediately after it in the same way. Running time $O(\alpha(n))$ amortized. One can also maintain in the UF sets such that each set consists of a free seat and the block of occupied seats immediately preceding it, so that the representative stores which seat is free (the maximal one), which slightly simplifies case handling.

We use an idea similar to Exercise 8 (binary heaps), Question 3(b). Maintain a binary max-heap of the smaller half of the elements and a binary min-heap of the larger half, together with their sizes. The keys in the max-heap are always smaller than those in the min-heap, and the size difference satisfies
\[
|{\rm max\text{-}heap}| - |{\rm min\text{-}heap}| \in \{0,1\},
\]
hence the median is always the maximum of the smaller half (constant-time access).

Initialization, insertion, deletion, and “closest to the median” queries are handled as follows:
- Initialization: find the median in linear time (median-of-medians), partition the array accordingly, and build the appropriate binary heap on each side; total linear time.
- Insertion/deletion: compare with the median and insert/delete in the appropriate heap. If the heaps become unbalanced (i.e., the size difference becomes 2 or −1), delete the root of the heap with more keys and insert it into the other heap. This performs a constant number of heap insertions/deletions, giving logarithmic time.
- Elements closest to the median: to return the \(k\) elements whose rank is closest to the median, use the algorithm from Homework 4, Question 3: from each binary heap, extract the \(k/2\) elements closest to its min/max, respectively, in sorted order in time \(O(k \log k)\); reverse the order of the small-side elements and merge.

Solution to the previous part: We use an idea similar to that in Exercise 8 (binary heaps), Question 3(b). We maintain a binary max-heap of the smaller half of the elements and a binary min-heap of the larger half, along with their sizes. We always maintain that the keys in the max-heap are smaller than those in the min-heap, and that the size of the max-heap minus the size of the min-heap is either 0 or 1 (therefore, the median is always the maximum of the smaller half—constant-time access). Insertion, deletion, and initialization proceed as follows:
\begin{itemize}
\item Initialization: find the median in linear time (median of medians), partition the array, and on each side build the appropriate binary heap; overall linear time.
\item Insertion/deletion: compare to the median and insert/delete in the appropriate heap. If the heaps become unbalanced (size difference 2 or $-1$), delete the root of the larger heap and insert it into the other. A constant number of heap insertions/deletions yields logarithmic time.
\item Elements closest to the median: to return the $k$ elements whose ranks are closest to the median, use the algorithm from Homework 4, Question 3: from each binary heap, extract the $k/2$ elements closest to the minimum/maximum in sorted order in time $O(k \log k)$, reverse the order of the smaller ones, and merge.
\end{itemize}

Solution to the current part: The same idea as in part (a), except we use AVL trees (which we cannot initialize in linear time) with pointers to the minimum and maximum, instead of binary heaps. Insertion and deletion are logarithmic, similarly.

This time, to return the elements closest to the median, we use what we learned in the exercise: one can output a sorted list of the $k$ smallest elements in an AVL tree with a pointer to the minimum in time $O(k)$ (and symmetrically for the largest) by accessing the minimum and then making $k$ successor calls.

Alternative solution: an AVL tree with fields \texttt{size}, \texttt{pred}, \texttt{succ} in every node and a pointer to the median. We can maintain predecessor and successor fields without hurting the complexity, because on each insertion and deletion we access the successor and predecessor in logarithmic time and update a constant number of pointers. The median can be found in logarithmic time after each insertion/deletion (this can also be done using only the number of elements in the structure together with the predecessor/successor fields).

To return the $k$ elements whose ranks are closest to the median, access the median and move $k/2$ steps backward (using the pointers), then move $k$ steps forward and output the elements.

Incorrect
The difference can be as large as $O(\log n)$. It is possible to construct a relatively small example in which the difference is $2$.

True
Place the minimal element at the root (depth 0). In its right child, place the second-largest element, and to its right continue placing the \(n/2\) largest elements along the right side of the chain. Keep the remaining \(\log n\) smallest elements, and arrange the rest of the elements on the right side.

Incorrect (circle). Let h denote the height/depth of the tree. Note that the number of nodes in the subtree of any node at depth 2 is at least $2^{h-3}$ (the level $h-1$ must be full). The total number of nodes in the tree is at most $2^{h}$ (one can bound this slightly better, but it is unnecessary). Therefore at least $1/8$ of the nodes must be greater than the $(n-\log n)$-th element. But $n-\log n < n/8$ for all sufficiently large $n$ (greater than $128$), and therefore this is impossible.

Incorrect
For example, a heap containing the numbers $1,\ldots,2^{10}$ contains only a single tree.

We use a dictionary implemented by a hash table with chaining of size \(O(n)\). Scan the array elements from left to right, and for each element \(x\): search for \(x\) in the dictionary; if it does not exist, insert it with \(info=1\), otherwise increase its \(info\) field by 1. At the end, the dictionary maps each element to its frequency in the array. Running time: \(O(n)\) in expectation, since each dictionary operation costs \(O(1)\) in expectation. Next, initialize a frequency array of length \(n\), where in cell \(i\) there is a linked list of all elements with frequency \(i\). Scan all dictionary records and insert each element in constant time into the linked list in the cell corresponding to its frequency (this can also be maintained during the construction of the dictionary). Finally, scan the frequency array from left to right and output the lists in order. Running time of the second part: \(O(n+n)=O(n)\) for scanning an array of length \(n\) that contains \(n\) elements across the lists. Total running time: \(O(n)\) in expectation.

Multiply the numbers by 1000 and round down, while storing the original value in the info field. Using radix sort, we can sort in linear time integer keys in the range $\{0, \dots, n^{101}\}$, as learned in class, by using base $n$ (if $n < 1000$ we can sort in constant time). Finally, use the info fields to reconstruct the original numbers. The desired property holds because if $b - a > 1/1000$, then $1000b - 1000a > 1$, hence $\lfloor 1000b \rfloor > \lfloor 1000a \rfloor$, i.e., the key we assigned to $b$ must appear after the key we assigned to $a$.

Find the median using the median-of-medians algorithm in time $O(n)$ and partition into elements larger than the median and those smaller than it. Recurse on each side with this operation. Perform this to depth $\log(n/r)$ to obtain blocks of size $r$ as required. Complexity: The algorithm’s operation can be described as a full binary tree of depth $\log(n/r)$. At recursion level $i$ there are $2^i$ disjoint groups, each of size $n/2^i$. Therefore, all median computations at the same level together take $O(n)$ time. Hence the total complexity is $O\!\left(n \log \frac{n}{r}\right)$.

Each solution can correspond to $(r!)^{n/r}$ possible inputs, since in each block there are $r!$ possible orders and there are a total of $n/r$ disjoint blocks. There are $n!$ inputs; therefore, the number of leaves in any comparison tree is at least $n! / (r!)^{n/r}$. But
\[
\log\!\left(\frac{n!}{(r!)^{n/r}}\right)
\;\ge\;
n\log n - O(n) - \frac{n}{r}\bigl(r\log r - O(r)\bigr)
\;\ge\;
n \log \frac{n}{r} - O(n)
\;=\;
\Omega\!\left(n \log \frac{n}{r}\right),
\]
as long as $r = o(n)$; otherwise, the bound is linear anyway.

We maintain two AVL trees, $T_1, T_2$, where $T_1$ contains the $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ smallest keys and $T_2$ contains the $\lceil n/2 \rceil$ largest keys. We keep pointers to the minima of $T_1, T_2$ and to the maximum of $T_1$. Each tree maintains a size field so that $\operatorname{select}$ is supported.

Insert: Join $T_1$ and $T_2$ (delete $\max(T_1)$ and use it to join the two trees) into a tree $T$. Insert the key into $T$. Using $\operatorname{split}$ at the median (found via $\operatorname{select}$), recreate the new $T_1, T_2$.

Delete: As in insertion. Delete the key from $T$, then split at the median to obtain the new $T_1, T_2$.

Search: Search in $T_1$ and $T_2$.

$\operatorname{select}(i)$: If $i \le \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$, perform $\operatorname{select}(i)$ in $T_1$; otherwise perform $\operatorname{select}(i - \lfloor n/2 \rfloor)$ in $T_2$.

The complexity follows from the fact that, given a pointer to the minimum, $\operatorname{select}(i)$ runs in $O(\log i)$ time.

Maintain an AVL tree with a size field. Implement all operations in a naive manner; only the amortized analysis is interesting. \texttt{insert}, \texttt{search}, \texttt{delete} — as usual. Running time: $O(\log n)$ in the worst case. \texttt{delete\_even\_rank} — delete, one after another, all keys of even rank (delete the second largest element, in the new tree delete the third largest element, and so on). Running time $O(n \log n)$ in the worst case. Amortized analysis: charge the cost of all operations (except \texttt{search}) to \texttt{insert}. Formally, by the banker's method, upon each insertion deposit $2 \log n$ coins into the bank. Of these, $\log n$ pay for the insertion itself, and the remaining $\log n$ will pay in the future, if needed, for deleting the inserted key (the deletion may occur either in \texttt{delete} or in \texttt{delete\_even\_rank}). Note: Clearly one can implement \texttt{delete\_even\_rank} in linear time. The current implementation is easier to analyze because it performs deletions that we charged to insertions.

\begin{itemize}
\item Initialization: Build two heaps, a max-heap and a min-heap, and additionally a variable named \texttt{multiple} initialized to 1. Traverse the array and initialize both heaps using it. During the build, also maintain cross-pointers between identical elements that appear in both heaps. Complexity: $O(n)$.

\item Multiplication by a constant: Given a constant $b$, update the field \texttt{multiple} as \texttt{multiple *= b}. Complexity: $O(1)$.

\item Find minimum: According to the sign of \texttt{multiple}, return the minimum/maximum of the relevant heap. When the sign is positive, return from the min-heap; otherwise, from the max-heap. Complexity: $O(1)$.

\item Delete minimum: Find the minimum (as above), then delete it from the relevant heap. Using the cross-pointer, locate the corresponding element in the other heap and delete it. Complexity: $O(\log n)$.

\item Insertion: Adjust the element by dividing by \texttt{multiple} (note that we may assume it is nonzero), then insert it into both heaps with cross-pointers. Complexity: $O(\log n)$.
\end{itemize}

Initialization: Build two heaps, a max-heap and a min-heap, and in addition variables named \texttt{multiple} initialized to 1 and \texttt{addition} initialized to 0. Scan the array and initialize both heaps from it. During the construction, also maintain pointers between identical elements that appear in both heaps. Complexity: $O(n)$

Multiplication by a constant: Given a constant $b$, update the fields \texttt{multiple} and \texttt{addition} as follows: \texttt{multiple *= b}, \texttt{addition *= b}. Complexity: $O(1)$

Addition by a constant: Given a constant $b$, update the field \texttt{addition} as follows: \texttt{addition += b}. Complexity: $O(1)$

Finding the minimum: According to the sign of \texttt{multiple}, return the minimum/maximum of the relevant heap. When the sign is positive, use the min-heap; otherwise, use the max-heap (remember to multiply the result by \texttt{multiple} and then add \texttt{addition}). Complexity: $O(1)$

Deleting the minimum: Find the minimum (as above) and then delete it from the relevant heap. Using the pointer, locate the corresponding element in the other heap and delete that element as well. Complexity: $O(\log n)$

Insertion: Adjust the key by $(\textit{key} - \texttt{addition})/\texttt{multiple}$ (assume $\texttt{multiple} \neq 0$), and then insert into both heaps with pointers. Complexity: $O(\log n)$

Data structure: a circular array with doubling. We maintain a global variable $M$ indicating the current array size, a global variable $n$ indicating the number of elements in the structure, and a global variable $last$ that points to the end of the queue.

Implementation of the operations:
- enqueue$(x)$: First, if $n = M$, copy the elements to an array of size $2M$ starting from index $1$ and set $last$ to $M+1$. Second, insert $x$ at position $last$, then increment $n$ and $last$ by $1$.
- dequeue(): The element to return is at index $(last - n)\%M$. Decrease $n$ by $1$. If $n = M/4$, copy the elements to an array of size $M/2$ starting from index $0$ and set $last$ to $M/4 + 1$.
- Retrieve$(i)$: Return the element at position $(last - n + i - 1)\%M$.

Space: We maintain $M/4 \le n \le M$, hence the space used by the data structure is linear in the number of elements stored in the structure.

Complexity analysis: All operations run in amortized $O(1)$ time. This can be proved using the potential function
$\phi(M,n) = \begin{cases}
2n - M, & n \ge M/2 \\
M/2 - n, & n \le M/2
\end{cases}$.

Data structure: We perform deamortization. At any moment we maintain three circular arrays of sizes $M/2$, $M$, and $2M$, where the active array that contains the queue is always the array of size $M$. We implement the operations as in the previous implementation with the following modification: if $n > M/2$, then in every operation we copy 1000 elements from the array of length $M$ to the array of length $2M$ (on the first operation we copy the first 1000 elements in the queue, then the next 1000, and so on). If $n < M/2$, then in every operation we copy 1000 elements from the array of length $M$ to the array of length $M/2$ (first the first 1000 elements in the queue, then the next 1000, and so on). When deleting an element in a dequeue operation, it must be deleted from all arrays in which it appears. If $n$ changes from larger than $M/2$ to smaller than $M/2$ or vice versa, we discard the copying work performed so far and restart it accordingly. If $n = M$, then we discard the small array of size $M/2$. The medium array becomes the small one, the large array (which already contains all the elements) becomes the medium one, and we allocate an array of size $4M$ to serve as the large array. A similar handling occurs in the case $n = M/4$. All operations run in worst-case time $O(1)$, and the space used by the structure is $O(n)$.

The classic trick for questions of this type is to isolate variables: $|a_j - a_i| = j - i$ if and only if either $a_j - a_i = j - i$ or $a_i - a_j = j - i$, i.e., either $a_j - j = a_i - i$ or $a_j + j = a_i + i$. Data structure: two AVL trees. One stores the $f$ smallest keys and one stores the $n-f$ largest keys. Initially (before any focus query) we have a single empty AVL tree. The trees maintain a size field for select queries. In addition, the tree with the smaller elements keeps a pointer to its maximum, and the other tree keeps a pointer to its minimum. We also keep a global variable $f$ that stores the “focus.”

Insert(key, val) — If $f$ has not yet been initialized, insert as usual into the single AVL tree of the data structure. Otherwise, compare key to the maximum of the tree of the $f$ smallest keys to decide into which tree to insert key. If we inserted key into the small-keys tree, delete its maximum there and move it to the large-keys tree.

delete(key) — If $f$ has not yet been initialized, delete as usual from the single AVL tree of the data structure. Otherwise, compare key to the maximum of the tree of the $f$ smallest keys to decide from which tree to delete key. If we deleted key from the small-keys tree, delete the minimum in the large-keys tree and move it to the small-keys tree.

setFocus($f$) — Merge the AVL trees and split them at the element of rank $f$. Update the global focus variable to $f$.

select($i$) — Perform select using the pointer to the minimum/maximum of the appropriate tree.

The classic trick in questions of this type is to isolate variables: $|a_j - a_i| = j - i$ if $a_j - a_i = j - i$ or $a_i - a_j = j - i$, i.e., if $a_j - j = a_i - i$ or $a_j + j = a_i + i$. Also note that the two conditions cannot hold simultaneously (which prevents double counting). Algorithm — we use two dictionaries implemented as hash tables of size $n$ with chaining. Denote the dictionaries by $D_1, D_2$. The value associated with a key counts how many times we have inserted that key. For $i = 1, \dots, n$: insert $a_i - i$ into $D_1$ with value 1 if it is not already there; otherwise, increment its value. Insert $a_i + i$ into $D_2$ with value 1 if it is not already there; otherwise, increment its value. Finally, iterate over all the values in the dictionaries, and for each value $x$ add $\binom{x}{2}$ to the answer. Build an array containing all endpoints of all segments. Mark each point as either a starting point or an ending point. First, sort the array so that the points are ordered from left to right. If there is a tie (i.e., equal points), place starting points before ending points. Second, scan the array while maintaining the number of starting points passed minus the number of ending points passed, simply by subtracting 1 when passing an ending point and adding 1 when passing a starting point. It is easy to see that this counter is exactly the number of segments that contain the current point; it is also easy to see that if there are 3 segments that share a common point, then there exists such a point that is, in particular, an endpoint of a segment. Therefore, return “yes” if and only if the counter reaches at some point a value of at least 3. The sorting takes time O(n\log n) and the scan takes linear time, so the total time is O(n\log n).

The algorithm:
We maintain an auxiliary heap. The auxiliary heap stores some of the keys of the original heap, with pointers to the corresponding nodes in the original heap. Initially we copy the root of the original heap into the auxiliary heap. At each step we perform \textsc{ExtractMax} from the auxiliary heap. We print the extracted element \(x\). We insert its two children (in the original heap) into the auxiliary heap. Repeat until \(k\) elements have been printed.

Correctness:
If the key of a node is not among the \(k\) largest keys, then it need not be printed, and consequently neither do its children. Note that at every step the auxiliary heap contains all candidates for printing, namely all the children (in the original heap) of the nodes that were printed. Any node whose parent is printed will be inserted into the auxiliary heap, and any node whose parent is not printed will not be inserted. Therefore the algorithm prints the \(k\) largest values, in fact in decreasing order.

Running-time analysis:
At each step we remove one element from the auxiliary heap and insert two in its place. Thus the size of the auxiliary heap is at most \(k+1\). Therefore a basic heap operation takes \(O(\log k)\) time.
Finding the two children is done in \(O(1)\) time (because we have a pointer to the corresponding node in the original heap). In total this contributes \(O(k)\).
In each step of the run we perform three operations on the auxiliary heap (one \textsc{Extract} and two \textsc{Insert}). There are \(k\) such steps. In total this is \(O(k \log k)\).
Therefore the overall running time is \(O(k \log k)\).
Note that in the last \(k/2\) steps the heap is already of size at least \(k/2\), hence a basic heap operation takes \(\Omega(\log k)\), so the total running time is also \(\Omega(k \log k)\), and therefore \(\Theta(k \log k)\).

Amortized running-time analysis for the structure:
Consider a sequence of $N$ operations. In such a sequence at most $N$ elements entered the structure (via Enqueue and PEnqueue).
If an element entered via PEnqueue (i.e., into stack $B$) then at most two operations are performed on it: Push to $B$ and possibly Pop from $B$.
If an element entered via Enqueue (i.e., into stack $A$) then at most four operations are performed on it: Push to $A$ and possibly Pop from $A$, and Push to $B$ and possibly Pop from $B$.
Thus, in any case we spend $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time per element.
Therefore, in any sequence of $N$ operations the running time is $\mathcal{O}(N)$. Clearly the running time is also $\Omega(N)$, since each operation in the sequence takes $\Omega(1)$.
Hence every sequence, and in particular the worst-case sequence, has running time $\Theta(N)$.
The amortized time per operation is therefore $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

Proof: The running time of \(Find(x)\) is the depth of the node representing \(x\). Each time the root of the tree to which \(x\) belongs is updated, the size of the set (the new tree) to which it belongs grows by at least a factor of 2. Hence, if the root of \(x\) was updated \(O(\log n)\) times, there are at least \(O(n)\) elements in the set. In other words, in a set of \(n\) elements the depth of a leaf in the tree cannot be more than \(O(\log n)\).

It is easy to see that if no \texttt{delete}s are performed, then the total cost is $O(m + k\log n)$. We perform an amortized analysis and show that even if \texttt{delete}s are performed, the cost remains the same. We use the banker's method: each \texttt{delete} deposits two coins. An expensive \texttt{delete}, one that rebuilds the tree, withdraws a number of coins equal to the size of the tree. Suppose we perform an expensive \texttt{delete} on a tree of size $x$, where $x/2$ of the nodes are marked as deleted. Then the operations that caused them to be marked as deleted deposited $2\cdot x/2 = x$ coins, and therefore we never go into overdraft.

In total, we obtain that the overall cost is $O(m + k\log n)$.

Proof: We show a lower bound of $\Omega(n\log n)$. Let $T(n)$ denote the worst-case running time of the best algorithm for the above problem under the comparison model. We show how to construct from such an algorithm a sorting algorithm: take an array of size $n$. Build a heap from it in worst-case time $O(n)$. Add the pointers $s$ in time $T(n)$. Find the minimum in time $O(1)$. Scan the heap using the pointers—start by printing the minimum, and at each step move to the next element via the pointer $s$ and print it. We obtain a sorting algorithm that runs in time $T(n)+O(n)$. All the operations we performed on the original keys are comparisons (including inside the BuildHeap algorithm). Therefore the algorithm fits the comparison model. By the bound shown in class, such an algorithm requires worst-case time $\Omega(n\log n)$. Hence necessarily $T(n)=\Omega(n\log n)$.

Common mistakes:
- Proving an upper bound instead of a lower bound (i.e., proposing a specific algorithm that solves the problem within the stated time).
- Ignoring the time to build the heap (the proof in class started from an array of $n$ elements, not from a heap that already satisfies some invariant).
- Proving only that it cannot be that updating every individual element takes $o(\log n)$, i.e., that there exists an element for which it takes longer. This does not imply that one cannot update all the elements in time $o(n\log n)$.

Many answers claimed “it’s like quicksort,” and that is not correct. In QS the number of comparisons at a level is always $n$, and the expected number of levels is $O(\log n)$. In contrast, here the expected number of comparisons per level is linear in $n$, whereas the number of levels is always $O(\log n)$ because the partition guarantees that there is at least a reduction of size at least $n/5$ (equivalently, one side is of size at most $4n/5$), which bounds the number of levels by $\log_{5/4} n$. We will use a red-black tree that supports the operations \operatorname{Insert}, \operatorname{Search}, \operatorname{Split}, \operatorname{Join} in O(\log n) time each.

Implementation of DeleteBetween:
1. Perform a split \(T_{<a},\, T_{\ge a} \leftarrow \operatorname{Split}(T,a)\), where \(T_{<a}\) is a red-black tree containing all elements less than \(a\), and \(T_{\ge a}\) is a red-black tree containing all elements equal to \(a\) or greater.
2. Now perform another split \(T_{[a,b]},\, T_{\ge b} \leftarrow \operatorname{Split}(T_{\ge a}, b)\), where \(T_{[a,b]}\) is a red-black tree whose elements are less than \(b\) and greater than or equal to \(a-1\), and \(T_{\ge b}\) is a red-black tree whose elements are greater than or equal to \(b\).
3. Finally, perform a join \(T \leftarrow \operatorname{Join}(T_{<a}, T_{\ge b})\) and return the tree that contains all elements except those between \(a\) and \(b\).

This uses a constant number of red-black tree operations, each taking \(O(\log n)\) time.

Init: Build an empty heap $H$, insert into it a bid of price zero for each product, and store the pointers in the array.

Bid: If $p$ is larger than the current bid for product $j$ (found in constant time via the pointer), then perform \textsc{Increase-Key} in $H$ for this product; otherwise ignore (we assume the product has not yet been sold). In a Fibonacci heap this costs $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized.

Sell: Perform \textsc{Delete-Max} on $H$ and return the product $j$ and its price $p$. There are at most $m$ bids in $H$, so this costs $\mathcal{O}(\log m)$ amortized.

Here we must keep all the bids because the same product type can be sold multiple times on different days. In addition to the heap \(H\) and the pointer array as in part (a), we maintain an array of \(m\) max-heaps (lazy binomial or Fibonacci). \(H_j\) contains all the bids pending for product type \(j\). The heap \(H\) contains the maxima (heads) of all the heaps \(H_j\), i.e., the best bid for each type.

Init: For every \(j\), build an empty heap \(H_j\) and insert a zero bid into it. Build \(H\) from the heads of the heaps and store pointers in an array.

Bid: Perform \(\text{Insert}(p)\) into \(H_j\). If its maximum changes (in particular, increases), perform \(\text{Increase-Key}\) as in part (a) to update the best bid for product type \(j\) inside \(H\). Overall \(O(1)\) amortized for the two operations.

Sell: Perform \(\text{Delete-Max}\) twice on \(H\) to identify the two product types \(j_1, j_2\) and their best bids \(p_1, p_2\), respectively. Perform \(\text{Delete-Max}\) also on the two heaps \(H_{j_1}\) and \(H_{j_2}\) to remove those bids, and insert into \(H\) the two new heads of these heaps, which are the next bids for these product types. Update the pointer array.

Total running time: \(O(\log n) = O(\log m) + O(\log n)\) amortized, since it is given that \(2m \ge n\), \(H\) contains at most \(m\) elements, and each \(H_j\) contains at most \(n\).

Complexity: $O(n) + O(m)$

We show how to compute the maximal overlap between a suffix of $s$ and a prefix of $t$; perform the same operation with the roles reversed and return the maximum of the two.

Build the suffix tree $T$ for $s$ in time $O(n)$. Now search for $t$ in $T$ by starting from the root and walking along edges as long as the characters of $t$ match the substring written on the edge (this is essentially searching for $t$ in the uncompressed trie).

On every visit to a node $x$ that has a leaf child $y$ and where the edge $xy$ is labeled only by the symbol $\$$, update $k$ to be the number of characters of $t$ read so far (since we have found an overlap between a prefix of $t$ and a suffix of $s$).

If we reach the end of $t$ in the middle of an edge and the only remaining symbol on that edge that we have not scanned is $\$$, then update $k$ to be $m$.

The scan takes $m$ steps, hence the total running time is $O(n) + O(m)$.

Note: One can obtain a running time of $O(\min(m,n))$ if we work with the prefix of $t$ and the suffix of $s$ of length $\min(m,n)$.

The algorithm we describe is a variation on Quick Sort.
Pick a random nut x and partition the bolts with respect to x. We now have the bolts larger than the nut, the bolts smaller than it, and the bolt equal to it, y. Partition the nuts with respect to y. Now continue recursively on the set of bolts larger than x and nuts larger than y, and on the set of bolts smaller than x and nuts smaller than y.
The recursion terminates when we reach \(n=1\).

The running time for the recursive calls is \(T(k-1) + T(n-k)\). Since \(x\) is chosen at random, the expected running time is
\[
\mathbb{E}[T(n)] \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} \bigl(\mathbb{E}[T(k-1)] + \mathbb{E}[T(n-k)]\bigr),
\]
and as we have seen for Quick Sort, the solution to this recurrence is \(\mathbb{E}[T(n)] = O(n \log n)\).

We show that any algorithm for the problem runs in time $\Omega(n \log n)$.
There are $n!$ possible ways to match the bolts to the nuts. Each comparison has one of 3 possible outcomes, hence the algorithm’s decision tree is a ternary tree. For the tree to have $n!$ distinct leaves, its height (both maximum and average) must be at least $\Omega(n \log n)$, namely at least $\log_3(n!)$.

Note: This cannot be proved by relying on the sorting lower bound seen in class, since the question does not ask to sort the nuts and bolts by diameter. Also, an example with a sorted array of nuts and an unsorted array of bolts does not establish an $\Omega(n \log n)$ lower bound, since the matching can be performed so that the outcome is not sorted; moreover, this is not a general instance of sorting—the fact that one array is already sorted provides a lot of information about the input.

Store the page numbers that are in the cache as a queue (a linked list ordered by recency—the most recent at the end of the queue) with a length field, and also keep a hash table that maps each page \(x\) in the cache to its list node in the queue.

\textbf{Init:} Initialize an empty hash table of size \(2n\) and an empty queue.

\textbf{Access:} If \(x\) appears in the hash, remove it from its current position in the queue (via its pointer) and insert it at the tail of the queue. If \(x\) does not appear in the hash, create a list node for it, insert it at the end of the queue, and store \(x\) in the hash table together with a pointer to the node.

If the length of the queue has risen to \(n+1\), delete the page \(y\) that is currently at the head of the queue from both the queue and the hash.

We used a constant number of operations in the hash table (each \(O(1)\) in expectation) and in the queue (each \(O(1)\) because we accessed the head, the tail, or via a pointer).

We use a balanced binary search tree augmented with auxiliary fields that are updated on every insert/delete: pointers to the minimal and maximal elements (found by walking only left or only right from the root down to a leaf), a pointer to the minimal element that is greater than or equal to the average, the number of elements in the tree, and the sum of the elements in the tree.
To maintain the pointer to the average element, upon insertion we update the count and sum fields, compute the average, and, using search, find the smallest element that is at least the average (i.e., the minimal element $\ge$ the average). The delete operation is handled similarly.
On calls to Min/Max/AVG we return the element indicated by the corresponding pointer.

We store at each node the size of its subtree. Upon a call to Stat, we climb the tree from the minimum element until we reach a node whose subtree size is at least \(k\). We then perform a recursive search down that subtree. At each step we compute the node’s rank as the size of its left subtree plus 1. By comparing to \(k\) we determine whether we are done or should continue to the right/left subtree. The tree is balanced, so the height of the smallest subtree of size at least \(k\) is \(O(\log k)\), which is the running time.
We maintain a pointer to the element with the aforementioned degree at each insertion/deletion using the operation from part~(b).

We maintain two balanced trees: the first stores the elements strictly smaller than the $\lceil N A \rceil$-th element by rank, and the second stores the rest. We maintain a pointer to the minimum of the second tree.

When calling StatPlus, we perform a search similar to part (b) that starts at the minimum of the second tree. The complexity, as in part (b), is $O(\log i)$.

Apply the select algorithm to find the order statistics of ranks \(\frac{i n}{5}\) for \(i=1,2,3,4\); we obtain four elements. Count how many times each of them occurs, and return an element that occurs at least \(\frac{n}{4}\) times. Complexity \(O(n)\).
See part (d). Complexity $O(n \log \log n)$.

Find the median of the array and count how many times it occurs in time $O(n)$. If it occurs at least $n/k$ times, return it. Otherwise, continue recursively on the two subarrays—one to the right of the median and one to its left. At stage $i$ the size of the array is $\lfloor n/2^i \rfloor$, therefore we will certainly stop when $2^i = k$. The total running time is $O(n \log k)$: the running time of each stage is $O(n)$ and there are at most $\log k$ stages.

We create an array of size $4$ that holds each of the types.
In each cell we maintain:

2. The maximal element in the list.

In addition, we maintain a field that holds the global maximum. Upon a call to \texttt{Insert} we access the appropriate cell in the array. We update the global maximum if needed. We subtract from the value of the inserted element the value stored in the offset field. We then insert it into the list of elements. Upon a call to \texttt{AddToElement} we simply update the element’s value and the maxima as needed. Upon a call to \texttt{max}/\texttt{maxAll} we return the value of the relevant max field. We maintain, for each type, a Fibonacci heap with an offset field, as well as a Fibonacci heap that contains the maximum of each type. In every relevant operation we maintain pointers from the maximum elements of the per-type heaps to the corresponding elements in the global heap. Now, in the operations \texttt{insert}/\texttt{addToType}/\texttt{addToElement} we operate on the appropriate per-type heap and, if necessary, update the corresponding element in the global heap via \texttt{increase-key}. We may also need to update the pointer. Similarly, for the \texttt{delete} operation: we delete the element from the appropriate heap, and if necessary delete it (using the saved pointer) from the global heap and insert into it the new maximum of the type. \texttt{Max}/\texttt{maxAll} return the maximum of a particular type’s heap or of the global heap. The running times of these operations on Fibonacci heaps meet the requirements.

Similarly to the previous parts. In addition, during the merge operation we update the maximum to be the maximum of the two maxima, concatenate the shorter list to the longer list, traverse the shorter list and, for each element, add its own offset and subtract the longer list’s offset. The final list is concatenated to \(t_1\). In \(t_2\) we set a pointer to an empty list. Deleting the maximum takes amortized \(O(\log n)\) time. Each such merge takes time on the order of the length of the shorter list, and the size of the combined list produced is at least twice that length. If we consider a particular element, the number of times we modify it over a sequence of merges is at most \(O(\log n)\). Therefore, the total number of modifications across all elements is at most \(O(n \log n)\).

a.
We will use an array with the deamortized resizing scheme learned in the amortization lecture—rather than waiting for it to fill and then moving everything at once to a larger array, we migrate the data in parts: every time we perform an insert after the current array is more than half full, we reserve two slots in the new array and move two elements to it.

\texttt{Insert}: As stated—we initially allocate an empty array of fixed size (e.g., 4). On every insertion after the array has become half full, we reserve two slots in the new array and move into them two elements, so that the current array fills up, the new array is exactly half full, and is ready for us to switch to it. A constant number of operations per insertion, hence $O(1)$ worst case (insertions are, of course, insert-last).

Since the only operations allowed are comparisons and assignments, we are in the comparison model. There are $n!$ possible permutations, hence the lower bound is the height of the decision tree, which is $\log(n!) = O(n \log n)$.

First, multiply all the numbers by $n^3$ to obtain $n$ numbers in the range $\left[0, n^3\right]$ whose pairwise distance is at least $1$. We can now round them (e.g., downward), and the distance guarantee ensures that we obtain $n$ integers ranging in $\left[0, n^3\right]$. We do this in a separate array and perform the same operations, in the same order, on the original array; thus we can sort the given numbers injectively. Now, as in the tutorial, view them in base $n$, so that each number has $3$ digits (each in $[0, n-1]$), and use radix sort, whose running time is $O(3(n+n))=O(n)$. All the other steps along the way are also $O(n)$.
Yes, since $n$ numbers are given, one cannot sort them without at least scanning all of them (which, as noted, takes $O(n)$).

Refutation: We reduce to the sorting problem. Assume that one can sort an array in less than \(O(n\log n)\), although we showed in class that this is a lower bound. Given an array, we scan all its elements, and for each one we find its insertion position in the tree and then insert it. It is known that a sequence of insertions into a WAVL tree when the insertion position is known costs \(O(\log\log n)\) amortized per operation; therefore, for \(n\) insertions we pay \(O(n\log\log n)\). After building the tree, we perform an in-order walk and output the elements to the array in sorted order in \(O(n)\). Altogether, the sorting complexity is \(O(n) + O(n) + O(n\log\log n) = O(n\log\log n)\), contradicting the lower bound we saw in class.

Maintain an array of size $m$, where each cell points to a max Fibonacci heap. \texttt{Init} — initialize an array of size $m$ and maintain a global max-heap. \texttt{Add\_bid} — access the appropriate array cell and insert into both heaps in amortized $O(1)$. \texttt{Highest\_bid\_for\_product} / \texttt{Highest\_bid\_overall} — return the values from the maximum pointers in amortized $O(1)$. \texttt{increase\_bid} — amortized $O(1)$. \texttt{retract\_bid} — amortized $O(\log n)$, similar to performing \texttt{delete\_min}.

\begin{itemize}
\item Maintain an array of size $m$, where each cell points to a Fibonacci max-heap.
\item \texttt{Init}: initialize an array of size $m$ and maintain a global max-heap.
\item \texttt{Add\_bid}: access the appropriate array cell and insert in amortized $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time into both heaps.
\item \texttt{Highest\_bid\_for\_product}/\texttt{Highest\_bid\_overall}: return the values from the maximum pointers in amortized $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time.
\item \texttt{increase\_bid}: amortized $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time.
\item \texttt{retract\_bid}: amortized $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ time, as in performing \texttt{delete\_min}.
\item \texttt{merge\_products}: analogous to heap union; merge the heap of product 2 into the heap of product 1 and update the maximum if necessary, $\mathcal{O}(1)$.
\end{itemize}

We use a hash function from a universal family. It is known that with hashing, insertion/search takes expected \(O(1)\). The key of each cell in the table is the product identifier, and the value points to a maximum Fibonacci heap.

\begin{itemize}
\item \texttt{Init} — initialize the hash table and maintain a global max heap.
\item \texttt{Add\_bid} — look up in the table and insert in \(O(1)\) amortized into both heaps.
\item \texttt{Highest\_bid\_for\_product} / \texttt{highest\_bid\_overall} — look up in the table and return the values from the maximum pointers in \(O(1)\) amortized.
\item \texttt{increase\_bid} — \(O(1)\) amortized.
\item \texttt{retract\_bid} — \(O(\log n)\) amortized, like performing \texttt{delete\_min}.
\item \texttt{merge\_products} — analogous to heap union: merge the heap of product 2 into the heap of product 1, update the maximum if needed; \(O(1)\).
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Algorithm description:}
Build a suffix tree for a string of length \(n\). Traverse the tree as follows—starting from the root, search for the string \texttt{ab}. If no path begins with \texttt{ab}, return \textbf{false}.
After that, continue traversing and search for the letters \texttt{b} and \texttt{a}. If we encounter the letter \texttt{a}, check whether the remainder of its edge leads to an internal node; if so, return \textbf{true}. If not, or if we do not find \texttt{a}, continue along the path indicated by the letter \texttt{b}.

\textbf{Running-time justification:}
Building the tree takes \(O(n)\).
Traversing the path in the tree that starts with \texttt{a} and then consists only of the sequence of letters \texttt{b} has length at most \(O(n)\), and each comparison along it is \(O(1)\), for a total of \(O(n)\).
At each step along the path we check whether there is a branch for the letter \texttt{a}, and whether it leads to an internal node—this is \(O(1)\) per step, over \(n\) steps, i.e., \(O(n)\).

Description of the data structures used for the implementation (and the purpose of each data structure):
We need to maintain three data structures: one to hold the board, one to hold the contents of the columns, and one to hold the contents of the rows. When I say “matrix,” I mean an \(n \times n\) two-dimensional array.
- For the board: maintain a matrix \(A\) such that the content of the board at position \((i,j)\) is \(A[i,j]\), and \(0\) denotes that the cell is empty.
- For the rows: maintain a matrix \(B\) such that if the number \(\text{num}\) appears in row \(i\), then \(B[i,\text{num}] = 1\), and otherwise \(0\).
- For the columns: maintain a matrix \(C\) such that if the number \(\text{num}\) appears in column \(j\), then \(C[j,\text{num}] = 1\), and otherwise \(0\).

Description of the operations:
- \(\text{Contains}(i,j)\): if \(A[i,j] \neq 0\), return \(A[i,j]\).
- \(\text{Write}(i,j,\text{num})\): if \(B[i,\text{num}] = 0\) and \(C[j,\text{num}] = 0\), set \(C[j,\text{num}] = 1\), \(B[i,\text{num}] = 1\), and \(A[i,j] = \text{num}\); otherwise, report that the operation is illegal.
- \(\text{Erase}(i,j)\): let \(\text{num} = A[i,j]\) and set \(B[i,\text{num}] = C[j,\text{num}] = A[i,j] = 0\).

Time and space complexity:
- Initialization: \(O(n^2)\), from allocating three two-dimensional arrays.
- Each operation runs in constant time, because there is a constant number of array accesses.

Similarly to the calculation for WAVL:
Let $s_k$ denote the minimal number of nodes in an $i$-WAVL tree whose root has rank $k$. Then
\[ s_k = 1 + 2 s_{k-1}, \]
a recurrence whose solution is
\[ s_k = 2^{\lvert k/i \rvert} - 1. \]
Hence, if the number of nodes in the tree is $n$, the root’s rank satisfies
\[ k \le i \cdot \log n. \]
In an $i$-WAVL tree it holds that $\text{height} \le \text{rank}$; therefore, this upper bound is also a bound on the height of the tree.
